title: practical english usage - Part 72
content:
typical of you/him, etc’ .

Would can be used to criticise a single past action, with the suggestion ‘ That's He was a nice boy, but he WOULD  talk about himself all the time.

Well, if you WILL keep telling people what you think of them .. .

She WILL fall in love with the wrong people.

Stressed will and would can be used to criticise people's behaviour.

3 criticisms:      She       fall    in   love     with     the    wrong       people.

Every summer  he would go away for a month, without saying where or why.

On  Sundays when I was a child we would all get up early and go fishing.

Sometimes he would bring me little presents without saying why.

Sulphuric acid will dissolve most metals.

Anything that can go wrong will go wrong.

If something breaks down and you kick it, it will often start working again.

When you look at clouds they will often remind you of animals.

She'll sit talking to herself for hours.

habitual or regular.

These verbs can also describe typical behaviour, often in the sense of what is 2  will    and   would In those days, a man might be hanged for stealing a sheep.

The flowers may have five or six petals, red or white in colour.

A female crocodile may lay 30-40 eggs.

In the days of sailing ships, a voyage round the world could take years.

Scotland can be very warm in summer.

Amy  can really get on your nerves.

could, and are common in scientific and academic language.

sense of what is possible. May and might are a little more formal than can and These verbs can be used to talk about typical behaviour and occurrences, in the 1 can,    could,      may     and   might will   , would 86 typical     behaviour:       can,    could,      may,     might, Section 7 Modal    Auxiliary      Verbs grammar •  87  typical behaviour: used to + infinitive not I lived in Chester for three years, (     l-itsedtolive inChes teefoMkree years;) not I worked very hard last month, (     I used to work very hard last month.) took, or how  many times it happened.

not used simply to say what happened at a particular past time, or how  long it now finished: there is an idea that circumstances have changed. The  structure is Used to refers to things that happened at an earlier stage of one’ s life and are 4 when     used      to  ... is  not   used not You used not to like him,  did you? (     .. .-usedyou?) These forms are not used in tags.

Used you to play football at school? (BrE) or / used not to like opera, but now I do. (   I used to not like opera ...) (BrE) not very common, and are rare in American English.

In a formal style, questions and negatives without do are possible, but these are I never used to like opera.

But the most common negative is never used.. .

/ usedn't to like opera.

The  contraction usedn't is also possible, especially in British English.

I didn't use(d) to like opera, but now I do.

What did people use(d) to do in the evenings before TV?

of did... use. Many people consider this incorrect.

When questions and negatives are written, they often have did.. . used instead 3 questions       and   negatives not lie smokes, (     He uses^o-smote) Her brother still collects stamps.

forms). To  talk about present habits and states, we  usually just use the simple Used to ... has no present form (and no progressive, perfect, infinitive or -ing 2 only    past past ► 000000000,9   0 θ      NOW] I used to smoke, but now I've stopped.

past    habits     and   states     which     are   now    finished That casino used to be a cinema.

We   use used to + infinitive to talk about past habits and states which  are now 1  meaning 87 typical     behaviou      r: used     to   + infinitive      __ grammar •  87  typical behaviour: used to + infinitive not   would    smoke I used to smoke. (     I                        .) not Robert used to play a lot of football. (     Robert would play...) behaviour.

And we  use used to, not would, to talk about regular and important habitual but not I used to have an old Rolls-Royce. (          I would have an old Rolls-Royce.) When we were children we used to / would go skating every winter.
